cues.x: $100M Leads, by Alex Hormozi.
Let me start with a promise, because this is a book built on one.
If you finish this walkthrough and actually do what it says, you will never be broke again.
That sounds like hype, but Hormozi means it in a very specific way.
He argues that the number one reason businesses fail, the number one reason good people with good products go under, is not that their product is bad.
It is that not enough people know the product exists.
If you can reliably get strangers to raise their hand and say I am interested, you have the one skill that solves almost every business problem.
Cash flow problems, hiring problems, even most product problems get easier when you have more leads than you know what to do with.
So the entire book is about one question, and it asks it over and over.
How do I get more people to show interest in the stuff I sell?
That is it.
That is the whole game.
Now let me set the foundation, because Hormozi builds on his first book.
In "$100M Offers," he taught you how to make an offer so good people feel stupid saying no.
That is the value side.
This book, "$100M Leads," is the attention side.
And he gives you the simplest possible equation for a business.
Leads plus offer equals a business.
You need people to talk to, and you need something worth talking to them about.
If you have a great offer but no leads, you starve.
If you have tons of leads but a weak offer, you struggle to convert.
Put them together and you have a machine.
This book is entirely about the first half of that equation, getting the leads.
Let me get the vocabulary precise, because Hormozi is a stickler about this and it matters.
A lead, in his definition, is simply a person you can contact.
That is the whole thing.
Not a customer.
Not even someone who wants to buy.
Just a human being you have a way to reach, an email, a phone number, a direct message handle, a face across a room.
That is a lead.
Then there is a more valuable creature he calls an engaged lead.
An engaged lead is a person who has shown they are interested.
They clicked.
They replied.
They downloaded the thing.
They booked the call.
They raised their hand.
So the job of advertising is to take leads, people you can contact, and turn them into engaged leads, people who have shown interest.
And advertising, he says, is just the act of making people aware of your stuff.
That is the skill.
Letting people know what you sell.
Master that and you are, in his words, unable to stay broke.
He is honest that he did not start out knowing any of this.
Hormozi's own story runs underneath the whole book, and it is worth hearing because it makes the frameworks real.
He started as a gym owner.
One location, then a chain of gyms, and he was broke and terrified, sleeping on an air mattress in his office at one point, down to almost nothing in the bank.
What dug him out was learning to get leads.
He turned his gym struggles into a gym turnaround business called Gym Launch, where he would fly in, fill a gym with new members using these exact methods, and take a cut.

Then he built a licensing model, then supplement and software companies, then Acquisition.com, a portfolio doing enormous revenue, hundreds of millions of dollars flowing through businesses he owns or backs.
And he is blunt that every single dollar traces back to solving this one problem, getting attention, getting leads.
So when he teaches you the frameworks, he is not theorizing.
He is handing you the ladder he personally climbed.
So here is the central framework of the entire book, the one everything else hangs on.
He calls it the Core Four.
And the claim is bold.
There are only four ways, four, to let another human being know that your stuff exists.
That is it.
Every ad you have ever seen, every cold email, every viral video, every referral, all of it collapses into just four buckets.
And once you see them as a grid, you can never unsee it, and you will never again wonder what to do to get more leads.
You will only wonder which of the four to do more of.
Let me build the grid for you in the air, because you cannot see it, so I want you to picture it.
Imagine a square divided into four smaller squares, two columns and two rows.
The columns are about who you are reaching.
The left column is people who already know you, your warm audience.
The right column is people who have no idea who you are, cold strangers.
Now the rows.
The top row is you reaching one person at a time, a private, one to one conversation.
The bottom row is you reaching many people all at once, a public, one to many broadcast.
Hold that picture.
Two columns, warm on the left, cold on the right.
Two rows, one to one on top, one to many on the bottom.
Now fill in the four boxes.
Top left, warm and one to one, that is warm outreach.
You personally reaching out, individually, to people who already know you.
Bottom left, warm and one to many, that is posting free content.
You broadcasting publicly to an audience that already follows or knows you.
Top right, cold and one to one, that is cold outreach.
You personally reaching out, individually, to strangers who have never heard of you.
And bottom right, cold and one to many, that is running paid ads.
You broadcasting publicly to strangers, and because they do not know you, you pay a platform to put you in front of them.
Warm outreach, posting content, cold outreach, paid ads.
Four boxes.
That is the Core Four, and Hormozi's promise is that if you are not getting enough leads, you are simply not doing enough of these four things, or not doing them well.
So let us walk each one in real depth, because the whole book lives inside these four.
Start with warm outreach, the top left box, warm and one to one.
This is the one almost everybody skips, and it is the one Hormozi says you should start with if you have nothing, no audience, no money, no reputation.
Because warm outreach costs nothing but effort and it works fastest.
The idea is simple and a little uncomfortable.
You reach out, one human at a time, to every single person who already knows you.
Your phone contacts, your email address book, your social connections, old coworkers, old classmates, friends, family, acquaintances, anyone who would recognize your name.
Hormozi's instruction is almost aggressive about the volume here.
Go through your entire contact list.
All of it.
Do not prejudge who might be interested.
Just work the list.
But the way you reach out matters enormously, because if you blast everyone with hey do you want to buy my thing, you will feel like a spammer and they will treat you like one.
So he gives you a posture and a script.
The posture is give, give, give, then ask.
You lead with value.
You are genuinely helpful, warm, and generous several times before you ever make a request.
And the conversational structure he teaches is called ACA, which stands for acknowledge, compliment, ask.
You acknowledge something specific and true about the person, you give them a genuine compliment, and then you ask a question that opens a door.
It feels like a real human reconnecting, because it is.
Let me make it concrete the way he does.
Say you sell fitness coaching.
You do not open with a pitch.
You reach out to an old friend and you say something like, hey, I saw you just ran that 10k, that is awesome, how did the training go.
That is acknowledge and compliment.
They reply, you have a real conversation, and somewhere in it you ask, are you still trying to hit any goals with your training right now, or are you happy where you are.
That is the ask, but notice it is a question about them, not a pitch about you.
If they say they are struggling to lose the last few pounds, now you have a natural opening.
You can say, you know, that is literally what I help people with, would it be crazy if I sent you something that might help.
Give first, ask second, and let the conversation earn the pitch.
Hormozi points out that even if only a small percentage say yes, when you work through hundreds of warm contacts, that is real revenue from zero dollars of ad spend.
And warm outreach is not just for the desperate beginning.
He still uses it, because a personal message from someone you know will always outperform a cold broadcast.
The lesson is do not be too proud or too shy to talk to the people who already like you.
Now the bottom left box, warm and one to many, posting free content.
This is you creating things, videos, posts, emails, podcasts, whatever, and putting them out publicly to an audience that follows you.
The magic of content is leverage.
Warm outreach is one message to one person.
Content is one piece of work that can reach a thousand or a million people, and it keeps working while you sleep.
But most people post content that goes nowhere, and Hormozi tells you exactly why and how to fix it with a three part structure.
Every good piece of content does three jobs in order.
Hook, retain, reward.
Hook is the first few seconds.
It is the headline, the opening line, the thumbnail, the thing that makes a scrolling person stop.
If you do not earn the stop, nothing else matters, because they never see it.
So you obsess over the hook, the callout, the promise, the curiosity, the bold statement that snaps attention.
Retain is the middle.
Once they have stopped, you have to keep them, so you cut the fluff, you keep the pace up, you deliver the good stuff quickly and keep them leaning in.
And reward is the payoff.
When someone gives you their attention, they must leave better off, entertained, taught, inspired, something.
If you reward attention, the platform shows you to more people and the viewer comes back for the next one.
Hook them, hold them, reward them, repeat.
Then he gives one of the most useful content rules in the book, and it answers the fear that stops people from posting, the fear of giving away too much.
His rule is give away the what and the why for free, and sell the how.
Tell people freely what they should do and why it matters.
That builds trust and proves you know your stuff.
But the how, the detailed step by step implementation, the hand holding, the done for you, the system, that is what you sell.
Think of it like a chef.
He will happily tell you the ingredients and why each one matters, that is the what and the why, and it makes you trust him.
But actually cooking the meal perfectly for you, that is the how, that is the restaurant.
Giving away the what and why does not cannibalize your sales.
It creates demand for the how, because people realize they want the result but do not want to do all the work themselves.
The other thing about content is that it compounds.
Hormozi is emphatic that content is a long game that pays off exponentially.
Your first hundred posts might feel like shouting into a void.
But you are building a skill, building a library, and building an audience.
Over time the audience grows, old content keeps getting found, and your reach snowballs.
The people who win are the ones who keep giving relentless value long after the point where quitters quit.
So the tactic is simple to say and hard to do.
Post valuable content consistently, structure every piece with hook, retain, reward, give away the what and the why, sell the how, and do it for far longer than feels reasonable.
Now cross to the right side of the grid, the cold column, strangers who do not know you.
And start with the top right box, cold and one to one, cold outreach.
This is reaching out individually to people who have never heard of you, cold email, cold DMs, cold calls.
It is the hardest of the four emotionally, because you will get ignored and rejected constantly.
But Hormozi loves it because it is direct, it is measurable, and it can start working today with no audience and no ad budget.
You just need a way to contact strangers and the stomach to do it at volume.
The first key to cold outreach is what he calls big fast value.
Because a stranger has no reason to trust you, you cannot lead with a small ask or a slow burn.
You have to lead with something so valuable and so immediate that even a skeptical stranger thinks, wait, this is worth my attention.
Give them something huge up front, a free resource, a specific insight about their business, a genuinely useful offer with no catch.
The bigger and faster the value, the more you overcome the natural coldness.
A weak cold message says, can I have fifteen minutes of your time to tell you about my services.
A strong one says, I made you a short video showing three things costing your business money right now, no charge, want me to send it.
One takes from them, the other gives to them.
The second key is personalization at scale, and this is the tension at the heart of cold outreach.
Personalized messages work far better, a stranger can tell instantly whether you actually looked at them or just blasted a template.
But truly custom messages take forever.
So Hormozi teaches you to find the sweet spot, ways to make each message feel individual while still sending huge volume.

That might mean a template with genuinely personalized openers, or grouping prospects by type so your big value offer is relevant to each group, or using small research shortcuts that let you drop in one specific true detail per person.
He frames the whole activity as one to one but at scale.
You keep the personal feel of a one to one message, but you build systems and use tools and sometimes a team so you can send hundreds or thousands of them.
And that brings the third key, which is volume, and Hormozi is almost brutal about it.
Cold outreach is a numbers game and the numbers are big.
If you send ten cold messages and give up, of course it did not work.
You may need to send hundreds or thousands to book a handful of calls.
He talks in terms of activity you might find shocking, hundreds of contacts a day.
The math only works at scale, so you have to commit to real volume, track your numbers, and treat every no as a step toward a yes.
And the fourth key ties to it, follow up.
The fortune is in the follow up.
Most people give up after one message, but most responses come after several touches.
So you follow up many times, politely and persistently, across channels, because the person who ignored your first message may reply to your fourth when the timing is finally right.
Big fast value, personalization at scale, sheer volume, relentless follow up.
That is cold outreach.
Now the last box, bottom right, cold and one to many, paid ads.
This is you paying a platform, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, Google, whatever, to put your message in front of a lot of strangers at once.
Paid ads are the most scalable of the Core Four, because you can turn a dial and reach more people almost instantly, and Hormozi has spent enormous sums here.
But they are also where you can lose money fastest if you do them wrong, so he is very tactical.
His first principle for ads is make the ad feel like content.
People are on those platforms to be entertained and informed, not to be sold to.
So an ad that screams buy now gets ignored, while an ad that hooks and rewards like a good piece of content earns attention and then makes the offer.
Remember hook, retain, reward from the content section.
It applies to paid ads too.
The best ads do not feel like ads.
They feel like something worth watching that happens to lead somewhere.
Then he gives a simple three part structure for what an ad actually needs to say, and you can hold it in your head.
Call out, who it is for, what to do.
First, call out, grab the attention of the right person, often by literally naming them or their problem, attention, gym owners, or, if you have lower back pain in the morning.
Second, who it is for and what you offer, quickly make clear this is for people like you and here is the valuable thing I have.
Third, what to do, a clear call to action, click here, sign up, book a call, get the free guide.
Call out the right person, tell them what you have got and that it is for them, tell them exactly what to do next.
Simple, but most ads fail because they skip one of the three.
Now the most important financial idea in the paid ads section, and honestly one of the most important in the whole book, is client financed acquisition.
This is the concept that lets you run ads forever without ever running out of money, and it is how Hormozi scaled so fast.
Here is the idea.
Advertising costs money.
Every customer you acquire has a cost, what he calls customer acquisition cost.

The trap most businesses fall into is that they acquire a customer, that customer slowly pays them back over many months, and in the meantime the business is bleeding cash to keep advertising, so growth is capped by how much cash you have.
Client financed acquisition flips this.
The goal is to get a new customer to pay you more, in their first thirty days, than it cost you to acquire them and to deliver your product to them.
In other words, within the first month, each customer not only covers their own acquisition cost but generates enough extra cash to pay to go get the next customer.
Let me make that concrete.
Say it costs you four hundred dollars in ads to get one customer, and it costs you a hundred dollars to deliver your service to them in the first month, so your total is five hundred dollars out.
If that customer pays you six hundred dollars or more in their first thirty days, you are now cash positive on day thirty, before they have even paid you the rest of what they will pay over the year.
That extra hundred plus dollars can immediately fund the next acquisition.
Now your growth is no longer limited by your bank account, because each customer literally finances the recruitment of the next one.
Hormozi calls this a self funding growth machine, and it is the difference between advertising being a scary expense and advertising being a money printer you feed and it hands more back.

To make it work you engineer your offer and your upfront pricing so that the first thirty days bring in enough cash, through a strong initial purchase, an upsell, a deposit, whatever fits your model, to cover acquisition plus delivery with room to spare.
The last paid ads tactic is testing many creatives.
You do not know in advance which ad will win, so you do not bet everything on one.
You make many versions, different hooks, different images, different angles, and you run them, you let the data tell you which ones win, you kill the losers and pour money into the winners.
Ads are an experiment, not a guess.
Test a lot, keep the winners, scale them.
That is paid ads.
So now you have the Core Four completely.
Warm outreach, one to one to people who know you.
Posting content, one to many to people who know you.
Cold outreach, one to one to strangers.
Paid ads, one to many to strangers.
And Hormozi's point is that this is the entire universe of getting attention.
You are never stuck for what to do.
You are only ever choosing which of the four to push.
He also suggests a rough progression for most people.
Start with warm outreach and content when you have no money, because they cost only effort.
Layer in cold outreach as you build a system.
And move into paid ads once you have an offer that converts and a handle on your numbers, because ads pour fuel on a fire that is already lit.
Do not pour fuel on no fire.
Before we go further, I want to make sure one tool is crystal clear, because it powers all four of the Core Four, and Hormozi devotes serious attention to it.
It is the lead magnet.
A lead magnet is the bait, the thing you offer for free to get a stranger or a warm contact to raise their hand and become an engaged lead.
And his definition is precise and worth memorizing.
A lead magnet is a complete solution to a narrow problem, given away free, that reveals the next problem, which your paid product then solves.
Let me unpack every word of that because each one is deliberate.
Complete solution.
It has to actually solve something, all the way, not a teaser that leaves them hanging and annoyed.
If you fully solve a small problem for someone for free, they think, wow, if the free thing was this good, the paid thing must be incredible.
That builds trust and goodwill.
Narrow problem.
It should solve one small, specific problem, not everything.
Narrow is good because it is quick to consume, quick to deliver value, and it is easy to make truly complete when it is small.
And here is the clever part, the part people miss.
Reveals the next problem.
A great lead magnet, by solving one problem, naturally exposes the next problem in the chain, the one your paid product is perfectly positioned to solve.
He gives the kind of example that makes it click.
Imagine a dentist offering a free teeth whitening.
It is a complete solution to a narrow problem, you want whiter teeth, here you go, done, and it feels great.
But sitting in that chair getting your teeth whitened, the dentist notices, and you become aware of, the cavity or the alignment issue, the next problem, which the dentist happens to fix for a fee.
The free thing solved something real and delighted you, and in doing so it surfaced the paid need naturally.
Or think of a nutrition coach giving away a free custom meal plan.
Complete, narrow, useful.
But now you have a plan and you realize you lack the accountability and coaching to actually stick to it, which is exactly what the paid program provides.
The lead magnet is not a random freebie.
It is the first step of a staircase, and the paid product is the rest of the stairs.
Hormozi lays out three types of lead magnets, and you can picture them as three flavors.
The first type reveals a problem the person did not fully know they had.
Think a free assessment, a quiz, an audit, an inspection.
A free posture assessment reveals that your back pain comes from a specific imbalance, which the paid physical therapy fixes.
It works because diagnosing a problem creates urgency to solve it.
The second type gives a free sample or trial, a taste of the actual product.
The free class at the gym, the free sample of the supplement, the seven day trial of the software.
It works because once people experience the value directly, many want to keep it.
The third type delivers one step of a multi step process.
If your paid product is a whole system with many steps, you give away one valuable step for free.
A free guide on the first phase of a home renovation, say, where doing that first step well makes them want you to do the rest.
All three follow the same logic, solve something real for free, and let it lead to the paid solution.
Then he gets tactical about how to make a great one and how to deliver it, because a lead magnet that is technically free but feels cheap or is a hassle to get actually hurts you.

On the make side, he says pick a real problem your ideal customer has, make sure your lead magnet actually solves it completely, and package it so it looks and feels valuable, give it a strong name, make it high quality, present it like it could have been something you charge for.
In fact he suggests you should feel slightly uncomfortable giving it away because it is so good.
That over delivery is what creates the wow.

On the deliver side, get it into their hands fast and smoothly, deliver it in a way that creates a great experience, and importantly, deliver it in a way that leads naturally to the next step, the ask, the offer, the call.

The whole point is that a great lead magnet, delivered well, creates two things at once, goodwill, they feel you gave them something genuinely valuable, and demand, they now want the thing that solves the next problem you just revealed.
Goodwill and demand.
That is a lead magnet doing its job.
Alright.
You now have the Core Four and the lead magnet that fuels them.
The natural next question is, how do I get more leads out of all this?
And Hormozi answers with a clean framework for scaling any of the Core Four.
He calls it More, Better, New.
Three words, three levers, and they are in order for a reason.
More means do more of what you are already doing.
This is the first and most overlooked lever.
Before you get fancy, before you add channels, just do more volume of the thing that is already working.
If you are sending fifty cold emails a day and getting results, send five hundred.
If you post once a week, post every day.
Most people, when leads slow down, immediately go looking for some new tactic, when the truth is they simply are not doing enough of the basic activity.
More is almost always the answer first.
It is boring, and it is boring because it works.
Better means improve your skill at the activity.
Once you are doing a lot, get better at it, so each unit of effort produces more.
Write better hooks so your content retains more people.
Improve your cold email so your reply rate doubles.
Sharpen your ad creative so your cost per lead drops.
Getting better means the same volume yields more leads, or the same leads cost less.
More is about quantity, better is about quality, and the two multiply each other.
New means add a new method.
Only after you have maxed out doing more and getting better at one Core Four activity should you add another one.
If you have been crushing content, now add paid ads.
If cold outreach is humming, now layer in a lead getter.
New is exciting, which is exactly why people jump to it too early, before they have wrung the value out of what they already have.
Hormozi's discipline here is important.
Master one channel, do more of it, get better at it, and only then add a new one.
Chasing new before exhausting more and better is how people end up mediocre at five things instead of great at one.
So whenever you want more leads, run the checklist in order.
Can I do more?
Can I do it better?
Should I add something new?
Almost always the answer starts with more.
Now we reach the second half of the book, and it is a genuine gear shift, because so far everything has been about you doing the advertising.
The Core Four are all things you do.
But there is a ceiling on you.
You only have so many hours.
So the most powerful move in the whole book is to get other people to advertise for you.
Hormozi calls these people lead getters, and this is how you go from a hardworking one person operation to a real machine that generates leads whether or not you personally lift a finger.
He identifies four kinds of lead getters, and just like the Core Four, this is a complete set.
Customers, employees, agencies, and affiliates or partners.
Let us walk each.
The first and most powerful lead getter is your own customers, through referrals.

When a happy customer tells other people about you, that is the single most trusted, most cost effective advertising that exists, because a personal recommendation carries more weight than anything you could ever say about yourself.
But Hormozi's key insight is that most businesses just hope for referrals passively, and that is leaving enormous money on the table.
Referrals should be engineered, deliberately built into how you operate.
Step one is make the product so good that people naturally want to talk about it.
There is no referral engine without a genuinely great result, because nobody stakes their reputation on recommending something mediocre.
So delivering an outstanding experience is the foundation.
Then step two, engineer the ask.
Do not wait and hope.
Build the referral request into your process at the moment the customer is happiest, right after they get a great result.
Ask directly, who else do you know that has this same problem, and would you be willing to introduce us.
Make it easy, make it specific, make it a normal part of the journey rather than an awkward one off.
Then step three, add referral incentives.
Give people a reason and a reward to refer.
That could be a discount, a bonus, a gift, cash, or a two sided reward where both the referrer and the new person get something.
Hormozi is a big fan of structuring incentives so that referring is a no brainer.
And he introduces the concept of the viral coefficient, which is the number you are really chasing.
The viral coefficient is how many new customers, on average, each existing customer brings you.
If every customer brings, on average, more than one new customer, something magical happens.
Your growth becomes self sustaining and explosive, because each new person brings more than one more, who each bring more than one more, and the thing compounds like a chain reaction.
Even getting that number up toward one, so that a large fraction of your growth comes free from referrals, dramatically lowers your cost of getting customers.
So you obsess over it.
What can I do so that each customer brings me more than one new customer?
Great product, engineered ask, smart incentives, and you watch the viral coefficient climb.
The second lead getter is employees.
This means hiring people whose actual job is to get leads.
Instead of you doing the cold outreach, you hire and train a team of people to do cold outreach.
Instead of you making all the content or running all the ads, you build a team that does it.
This is how you scale the Core Four beyond your own two hands.
Hormozi is realistic that this is a real transition.
You have to learn to hire well, to train people to do what you do, to hold them accountable to numbers, and to lead them.
But the payoff is leverage.
Ten trained people doing warm and cold outreach generate ten times the activity you could alone.

He emphasizes that you should first master an activity yourself so you deeply understand it, then document it, then hire and train others to run it, so you are teaching from real knowledge rather than handing over something you never figured out.
Your job shifts from doing the advertising to building and leading the team that does the advertising.
The third lead getter is agencies.
An agency is a company you pay to do your advertising for you, experts who already know how to run ads or do outreach or produce content.
Hormozi's take on agencies is nuanced and useful.
He does not say avoid them or worship them.
He says use them strategically, especially to learn.
A good agency can get you results faster than fumbling alone, and just as valuably, you can learn how they do it.

His recommended posture is to hire an agency to run a channel, watch closely, learn everything you can about how they get results, and use that knowledge either to keep scaling with them or to eventually build the capability in house with your own employees.
He warns you not to be completely dependent and ignorant, handing over money and hoping, because then you never build the skill and you are at their mercy.
Treat the agency as both a service and a masterclass.
Get leads now, and get educated for later.
The fourth lead getter is affiliates and partners.
These are other businesses or individuals who promote your stuff to their audience in exchange for a cut, usually a commission on the sales they drive.
The beauty here is that an affiliate or partner already has the trust and the audience you want to reach, and you only pay them when they actually produce a result, so it is low risk.
Think about someone who serves the same customers you do but is not a competitor.
A wedding photographer and a wedding venue.
A nutrition coach and a gym.
You can set up an arrangement where they send their people to you and you reward them for it.

Hormozi walks through building these relationships, making it worth the partner's while, giving them the materials and the incentive to promote you, and treating the partnership as a real relationship you nurture, not a transaction you set and forget.
Done well, a handful of good affiliate partners can send you a steady stream of pre trusted leads for a fraction of what cold acquisition would cost.
And then Hormozi zooms out on the lead getters and makes the crucial leadership point, because this is where the game changes for you personally.
Building an army of lead getters means your role transforms.
You are no longer primarily an advertiser.
You become the leader of advertisers.
Your job becomes recruiting, training, motivating, and managing the people and partners who get leads for you.

He talks about how to build and lead this team, setting clear expectations and clear numbers everyone is accountable to, giving them the tools and training and offers they need to succeed, rewarding performance well so your best lead getters stay and grow, and creating a culture where getting leads is understood as the lifeblood of the business.

The ultimate vision of the book is a business where leads flow in from many sources at once, some from your own Core Four activities, and much of it from an ecosystem of customers referring, employees prospecting, agencies executing, and partners promoting, all pointed at your offer.
That is what a hundred million dollar lead machine actually looks like.
Not one heroic person hustling, but a system of many people and channels all generating attention.
Let me tie Hormozi's own journey to all of this one more time, because it makes the frameworks land.

When he was a broke gym owner, he had no money for ads, so he lived in the warm and free quadrants, warm outreach and content and hustle, plus lead magnets like free workouts and challenges to get people in the door.

As he learned to convert, he layered in paid ads, and it was client financed acquisition that let him scale a gym from struggling to full without needing a fortune, because each new member's first payments funded getting the next member.

When he built Gym Launch, he taught these exact lead magnet and Core Four systems to hundreds of gyms, and then he became the ultimate lead getter operator, using affiliates, a trained team, and referrals to scale wildly.

Everything he built, the gyms, the licensing company, the supplement brand, the software, the portfolio at Acquisition.com doing enormous numbers, all of it is the Core Four plus lead magnets plus More Better New plus lead getters, running at scale.
He is not selling you a theory.
He is reverse engineering the actual path he walked from an air mattress to hundreds of millions of dollars, and telling you the path is repeatable because the mechanics are always the same.
So let me pull the whole thing together into one clear picture, because I want you to be able to hold this entire book in your head as you drive or lift.

The goal is leads, engaged leads, people who have shown interest, because leads plus offer equals a business, and advertising, simply letting people know your stuff exists, is the skill that means you never stay broke.
To get those leads there are only four ways, the Core Four, and you can see them as a two by two grid.
Warm and one to one is warm outreach.
Warm and one to many is content.
Cold and one to one is cold outreach.
Cold and one to many is paid ads.

Each one you power with a lead magnet, a complete solution to a narrow problem, given free, that reveals the next problem your paid product solves, and that creates goodwill and demand at the same time.
To scale any of the four you pull three levers in order, More, do more volume, Better, improve your skill, New, add a channel, and you always start with more.

And then you multiply everything by getting other people to advertise for you, the four lead getters, customers who refer, employees you hire, agencies you rent and learn from, and affiliates and partners who promote you for a cut, all of it turning you from an advertiser into the leader of advertisers.
Now the most important part, because a book you merely understand changes nothing.
Hormozi ends where I want to end you, on action, and he is almost stern about it.
Knowing the Core Four is worthless if you do not go do them.
So here is the one thing to do today, and it is deliberately simple.
Pick one of the Core Four, just one, the one you can start right now with what you have, and do far more of it than you are doing today.
If you have no money and no audience, that is warm outreach, so go open your contacts right now and message ten people using acknowledge, compliment, ask, giving before you ask.
If you have a little audience, post a piece of content built on hook, retain, reward, and commit to doing it daily.
If you have a product and some cash, build one strong lead magnet and put a simple paid ad behind it with call out, who it is for, what to do.
Just pick one and flood it with volume.
Because here is the quiet truth underneath this whole book.
You do not have a lead problem.
You have a volume problem and an action problem.
The frameworks are not complicated.
There are only four ways to get attention, and you already know all four now.
The people who win are not the ones with secret tactics.
They are the ones who pick a lane and do an almost embarrassing amount of it, consistently, for longer than everyone else is willing to.
So do not close this out and feel informed.
Close it out and go do the reps. Advertise.
Let people know what you have got.
Do more of it than feels reasonable, get a little better each week, and when you have squeezed one channel dry, add the next.
Do that, and Hormozi's promise stops being a slogan and becomes your actual life.
More leads than you can handle, and a business that never has to be broke again.
Now go pick your one, and go do far more of it, starting today.
